title: Three tours in one week
summary: A tour guide notices the same elderly tourist on three of her tours in one week. She asks him why. His answer changes what she will say on tomorrow's tour.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: Mr Kostas, you have been on my tour three times this week. Different days. Is something wrong?

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: Nothing is wrong. I am sorry. I will pay for all three.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: I do not mean that. I mean, what are you looking for? I tell the same stories. You have heard them now three times.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: I am looking for the moment in your story when you say 'and this is home.'

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: I do not say that.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: No. But you say something like it. When you talk about the corner where the old woman sells sticky rice. The way you say her name. I have heard you say it three times. It is the way someone says the name of their home, without thinking about it.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: I have lived in this city for twenty-eight years.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: I have lived nowhere for twenty-eight years. I lost my home in 1996. I have lived in five countries since. I am seventy now. I will not live somewhere long enough to call it home.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: I am sorry.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: Do not be. I am not telling you to feel sorry for me. I am telling you why I take your tour three times. Your tour is one of the few places where I hear someone say the name of their home without thinking about it. I am studying it.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: Studying what?

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: The voice. The exact tone. I want to learn how a person sounds when a place is theirs. So I can imagine what I sound like when I am at home, even though I am not.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: That is the saddest reason for taking a tour I have ever heard.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: Possibly. But it is useful to me. When I go back to my hotel and call my granddaughter, I try the tone on the name of our old town. She is six. She does not know I am acting.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: She is six? Where is she?

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: In Berlin. She has never seen our town. I have a photograph. She has the photograph. The town is gone. We are not gone.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: Mr Kostas, please come on tomorrow's tour. Free. I will say the name of the corner woman three times tomorrow, slowly, so you hear it well.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: Thank you. Then I will not need to come on Sunday.

body.speaker: Tam
body.text: Bring a small recorder. I will sound natural. I cannot help it. Twenty-eight years is enough to make a person sound at home, even if she was not born here.

body.speaker: Mr Kostas
body.text: That is what I am hoping.

grammarNotes.title: Two present-perfect sentences placed side by side
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Văn nói C1/B2 thường đặt hai câu hiện tại hoàn thành cạnh nhau để tạo tương phản. Trong bài: 'I have lived in this city for twenty-eight years.' / 'I have lived nowhere for twenty-eight years.' Cùng cấu trúc, cùng khoảng thời gian, nghĩa hoàn toàn đối lập — một cách rất mạnh để nói lên kinh nghiệm của ông Kostas.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Two present-perfect sentences placed side by side create a strong contrast. From the dialogue: 'I have lived in this city for twenty-eight years.' / 'I have lived nowhere for twenty-eight years.' Same structure, same period, opposite meaning — a powerful way to state Mr Kostas's situation.

cloze.template:
Tam: Mr Kostas, you have been on my tour {{b1}} times this week. Different days. Is something wrong?
Mr Kostas: Nothing is wrong. I am sorry. I will pay for all three.
Tam: I do not mean that. I mean, what are you looking for? I tell the same {{b2}}. You have heard them now three times.
Mr Kostas: I am looking for the moment in your story when you say 'and this is {{b3}}.'
Tam: I do not say that.
Mr Kostas: No. But you say something like it. When you talk about the corner where the old woman sells {{b4}} rice. The way you say her {{b5}}. I have heard you say it three times. It is the way someone says the name of their home, without thinking about it.
Tam: I have lived in this city for {{b6}} years.
Mr Kostas: I have lived nowhere for twenty-eight years. I lost my home in {{b7}}. I have lived in {{b8}} countries since. I am {{b9}} now. I will not live somewhere long enough to call it home.
Tam: I am sorry.
Mr Kostas: Do not be. I am not telling you to feel sorry for me. I am telling you why I take your tour three times. Your tour is one of the few places where I hear someone say the name of their home without thinking about it. I am studying it.
Tam: Studying what?
Mr Kostas: The {{b10}}. The exact {{b11}}. I want to learn how a person sounds when a place is theirs. So I can imagine what I sound like when I am at home, even though I am not.
Tam: That is the {{b12}} reason for taking a tour I have ever heard.
Mr Kostas: Possibly. But it is useful to me. When I go back to my hotel and call my {{b13}}, I try the tone on the name of our old town. She is six. She does not know I am acting.
Tam: She is six? Where is she?
Mr Kostas: In {{b14}}. She has never seen our town. I have a {{b15}}. She has the photograph. The town is gone. We are not gone.
Tam: Mr Kostas, please come on tomorrow's tour. Free. I will say the name of the corner woman three times tomorrow, slowly, so you hear it well.
Mr Kostas: Thank you. Then I will not need to come on Sunday.
Tam: Bring a small {{b16}}. I will sound natural. I cannot help it. Twenty-eight years is enough to make a person sound at home, even if she was not born here.
Mr Kostas: That is what I am hoping.

blanks.explanation: He has been on her tour three times this week.
Kostas is listening for the moment Tam says 'and this is home.'
The corner woman sells sticky rice.
It is the way Tam says her name.
Tam has lived in the city for twenty-eight years.
He has lived in five countries since.
The exact tone — how a person sounds when a place is theirs.
Tam: 'the saddest reason for taking a tour I have ever heard.'
He has a photograph of their old town.
Tam tells him to bring a small recorder.

translationVi:
Tâm: Bác Kostas, tuần này bác đi tour của tôi ba lần rồi. Vào những ngày khác nhau. Có chuyện gì không ạ?
Ông Kostas: Không có chuyện gì. Xin lỗi cô. Tôi sẽ trả tiền cho cả ba lần.
Tâm: Tôi không có ý đó. Tôi muốn hỏi bác đang tìm cái gì. Tôi kể cùng những câu chuyện. Bác đã nghe ba lần.
Ông Kostas: Tôi đang tìm khoảnh khắc trong câu chuyện của cô khi cô nói 'và đây là nhà của tôi.'
Tâm: Tôi không nói câu đó.
Ông Kostas: Không. Nhưng cô nói một thứ gần giống. Khi cô kể về cái góc phố nơi bà cụ bán xôi. Cách cô gọi tên bà. Tôi đã nghe cô gọi ba lần. Đó là cách một người gọi tên nhà mình, mà không cần suy nghĩ.
Tâm: Tôi đã sống ở thành phố này hai mươi tám năm.
Ông Kostas: Tôi đã không có chỗ nào để gọi là nhà suốt hai mươi tám năm. Tôi mất nhà năm 1996. Từ đó tôi sống ở năm nước. Giờ tôi bảy mươi. Tôi sẽ không sống ở đâu đủ lâu để gọi đó là nhà.
Tâm: Tôi xin lỗi.
Ông Kostas: Đừng. Tôi không kể để cô thấy tội. Tôi kể vì sao tôi đi tour của cô ba lần. Tour của cô là một trong vài nơi tôi nghe ai đó gọi tên nhà mình mà không cần suy nghĩ. Tôi đang nghiên cứu.
Tâm: Nghiên cứu cái gì ạ?
Ông Kostas: Giọng. Đúng cái ngữ điệu. Tôi muốn học cách một người vang lên khi nơi đó là của họ. Để tôi có thể tưởng tượng giọng tôi sẽ như thế nào khi tôi ở nhà, dù tôi đang không ở.
Tâm: Đó là lý do buồn nhất tôi từng nghe để đi một tour.
Ông Kostas: Có thể. Nhưng có ích với tôi. Khi tôi về khách sạn và gọi cháu gái tôi, tôi thử ngữ điệu đó với tên thị trấn cũ của chúng tôi. Cháu sáu tuổi. Cháu không biết tôi đang diễn.
Tâm: Cháu sáu tuổi? Cháu ở đâu?
Ông Kostas: Ở Berlin. Cháu chưa từng thấy thị trấn của tôi. Tôi có một bức ảnh. Cháu có bức ảnh đó. Thị trấn đã mất. Chúng tôi vẫn còn.
Tâm: Bác Kostas, mai bác lại đi tour của cháu nhé. Miễn phí. Mai cháu sẽ gọi tên bà cụ ở góc phố ba lần, chậm rãi, để bác nghe rõ.
Ông Kostas: Cảm ơn cô. Vậy Chủ Nhật tôi không cần đi nữa.
Tâm: Bác mang theo cái máy ghi âm nhỏ. Cháu sẽ nghe tự nhiên thôi. Không nhịn được đâu. Hai mươi tám năm là đủ để một người vang lên như đang ở nhà, dù cô ấy không sinh ra ở đây.
Ông Kostas: Đó là điều tôi đang hy vọng.

questions.prompt: Why has Tam stopped Mr Kostas at the start?
options: He has been on her tour three times this week and she wonders why
questions.explanation: 'You have been on my tour three times this week.'

questions.prompt: What is Mr Kostas actually listening for on the tour?
options: The moment when Tam — without thinking — says the name of her home
questions.explanation: 'I am looking for the moment in your story when you say "and this is home."'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Kostas's first long turn.

questions.prompt: How does Mr Kostas describe the way Tam says the sticky-rice woman's name?
options: It is the way someone says the name of their home, without thinking about it
questions.explanation: 'It is the way someone says the name of their home, without thinking about it.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Kostas's turn about the corner.

questions.prompt: What is the powerful contrast in their two statements about twenty-eight years?
options: Tam has lived in this city for twenty-eight years; Mr Kostas has lived nowhere for twenty-eight years
Both have lived in five countries
questions.explanation: 'I have lived in this city for twenty-eight years.' / 'I have lived nowhere for twenty-eight years.'
questions.hint: Look at the two adjacent turns.

questions.prompt: When did Mr Kostas lose his home, and how old is he now?
questions.explanation: 'I lost my home in 1996.' / 'I am seventy now.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Kostas's long reply.

questions.prompt: What is Mr Kostas studying on the tour?
options: The voice — the exact tone — of a person speaking the name of their home
questions.hint: Look at Mr Kostas's turn after 'Studying what?'

options: So he can imagine what he sounds like when he is at home, even though he is not
questions.explanation: 'So I can imagine what I sound like when I am at home, even though I am not.'

questions.prompt: Who does Mr Kostas call from his hotel, and why?
options: His six-year-old granddaughter; he tries the tone on the name of their old town
questions.explanation: 'I call my granddaughter, I try the tone on the name of our old town.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about the hotel.

questions.prompt: Where does his granddaughter live, and has she seen the town?
options: Berlin; she has never seen the town
questions.explanation: 'In Berlin. She has never seen our town.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about her location.

questions.prompt: What do he and his granddaughter both have?
options: A photograph of the old town
questions.explanation: 'I have a photograph. She has the photograph.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Kostas say about the town and themselves?
options: The town is gone. We are not gone.
He is gone but the town remains
questions.explanation: 'The town is gone. We are not gone.'

questions.prompt: What does Tam offer at the end?
options: Tomorrow's tour for free, with the corner woman's name said three times slowly
questions.explanation: 'Please come on tomorrow's tour. Free. I will say the name of the corner woman three times tomorrow, slowly, so you hear it well.'

questions.prompt: What does Tam tell Mr Kostas to bring?
options: A small recorder — she will sound natural; twenty-eight years is enough to make a person sound at home
questions.explanation: 'Bring a small recorder. I will sound natural. I cannot help it.'

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to the dialogue's central idea?
options: Home is partly something that lives in the way a person speaks; it can be carried into other lives by tone, even by tourists studying tour guides.
Refugees should forget their old homes.
Tour guides should be paid more.
questions.explanation: The whole exchange turns on Mr Kostas studying Tam's tone of voice for a sound he cannot otherwise have.
questions.hint: Look at his explanation of why he is on the tour.

questions.prompt: What is the tone between Tam and Mr Kostas?
options: Honest, gentle, and a little funny — they meet as strangers and end as something else
questions.explanation: He confides, she offers a free tour with a small joke; he accepts with a real thank you.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Kostas says he is studying 'how a person sounds when a place is theirs.' Think of the way you say the name of where you live. What is in your tone that you did not put there on purpose? What is in it that you would miss if it were taken away?
